title: When AI Infrastructure Is Optional but Governance Lock-In Is Not
subtitle: An AI-SNI Local Governance Diagnostic of the Temple (GA) Data Center Proposal
title_normalized: when ai infrastructure is optional but governance lock-in is not: an ai-sni local governance diagnostic of the temple (ga) data center proposal

creators.name: Wu, Shaoyuan
creators.affiliation: Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC

publisher: Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC

alternate_identifiers.description: EPINOVA Working Paper D-Series publication number

description: This working paper applies the AI-Strategic Node Index (AI-SNI) as a local governance diagnostic to the proposed Project Bus data center campus in Temple, Georgia. It distinguishes commercially viable AI-enabling infrastructure from structurally necessary AI system nodes and argues that the proposal shows limited evidence of non-substitutability while generating governance friction and path-dependency risk.
abstract: As artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure rapidly expands at sub-national levels, local governments are increasingly asked to approve large-scale data centers under claims of strategic necessity, digital competitiveness, or AI leadership. However, existing evaluation frameworks rarely distinguish between commercially viable infrastructure and structurally necessary AI system nodes. This article applies the AI-Strategic Node Index (AI-SNI), a governance-oriented diagnostic framework originally developed for macro-strategic analysis, to a local infrastructure controversy: the proposed data center campus ("Project Bus") in Temple, Georgia. The analysis demonstrates that while the proposed facility may hold commercial or optional future value, it does not constitute a demonstrably structurally necessary AI node under current evidentiary conditions. Instead, the project exhibits high governance friction and long-term path-dependency risks disproportionate to its demonstrated system indispensability.

dates.description: First public release of EPINOVA Working Paper D-Series No. 2026-01.

files.label: Download PDF
files.filename: When AI Infrastructure Is Optional but Governance Lock-In Is Not.pdf

series_information.series_description: WP-D examines governance mechanisms and institutional design in AI-mediated systems, focusing on how infrastructure, authority, and decision processes generate lock-in, coordination burden, and failure risk and how such risks can be structurally contained.

methodology.case: Temple, Georgia data center proposal (Project Bus)
scoring_assumptions: Equal weighting across five AI-SNI dimensions (α1–α5 = 0.2)
Evidence-bounded scoring with explicit uncertainty recognition
Reference composite score only; no alternative aggregation formulations
methodology.core_diagnostic_conclusion: The proposed Temple data center does not satisfy AI-SNI criteria for structural necessity or non-substitutability within AI-mediated systems. Its primary risk lies in governance friction and premature infrastructural lock-in rather than in demonstrated system indispensability.

dimensions.name: Sensing and Monitoring Centrality
dimensions.interpretation: No exclusive geographic, environmental, or system-level sensing function is associated with the proposed site; the AI system does not acquire uniquely situated perceptual or monitoring inputs from the Temple node.

dimensions.name: Predictive and Modeling Dependency
dimensions.interpretation: No demonstrated binding exists between the proposed facility and specific predictive models or domain-critical forecasting systems; compute location remains functionally fungible under current evidence.

dimensions.name: Decision Pipeline Criticality
dimensions.interpretation: The facility may function as a general-purpose compute supply node but does not operate as a non-bypassable execution, authorization, or decision-trigger junction.

dimensions.name: Governance and Control Alignment
dimensions.interpretation: Rezoning, annexation, multi-jurisdictional governance interfaces, community opposition, and emerging state-level scrutiny create disproportionate governance complexity relative to demonstrated structural necessity.

dimensions.name: Future Coupling and Path Dependency
dimensions.interpretation: The project creates future options but also introduces elevated risk of premature infrastructural lock-in where platform binding, regulatory durability, and exit costs remain insufficiently specified.

key_findings: The proposed Temple data center does not constitute a structurally necessary AI node.
Across D1–D3, the project does not demonstrate non-substitutable roles in AI-mediated sensing, prediction, or decision pipelines.
Governance friction, rather than technical insufficiency or capacity shortage, is the primary structural risk associated with the proposed facility.
Future coupling potential does not convert into justified structural necessity without demonstrated platform binding, regulatory durability, or credible exit pathways.
Local authorities should require explicit demonstrations of structural necessity before approving large-scale, difficult-to-reverse AI infrastructure projects.

tables.title: AI-SNI Dimension-Level Scores for the Temple (GA) Data Center Proposal
tables.description: Reports dimension-level diagnostic scores for D1 through D5: D1 = 0.12, D2 = 0.43, D3 = 0.47, D4 = 0.35, and D5 = 0.53, producing a composite AI-SNI score of approximately 0.38.
tables.source: Author’s AI-SNI assessment based on publicly available information and evidence-bounded scoring
